seoTitle: How to Clean Data in Power BI Using Power Query
metaDescription: Learn the Power Query essentials for cleaning data in Power BI, including filtering, removing duplicates, and transforming datasets.
intro: Clean data is the foundation of any reliable Power BI report. This tutorial covers the Power Query essentials for cleaning data, from filtering rows to removing duplicates, so your visuals are built on accurate, analysis-ready datasets.

steps.heading: Open Power Query
steps.body: Navigate to Power Query within Power BI to access the data transformation tools before your data reaches the report canvas.

steps.heading: Filter your dataset
steps.body: Remove rows that don't belong in your analysis, narrowing the dataset down to what's relevant.

steps.heading: Remove duplicates
steps.body: Use Power Query's duplicate removal tools to eliminate repeated records that would otherwise skew your totals.

steps.heading: Transform your data
steps.body: Apply transformations such as splitting columns, changing data types, or renaming fields to standardize the dataset.

steps.heading: Load the cleaned data into your report
steps.body: Once cleaned, load the dataset into your report so it's ready for accurate visualization and analysis.

faq.question: Does cleaning data in Power Query change my original data source?
faq.answer: No, Power Query transformations are applied to a copy of the data used within Power BI, leaving the original source untouched.

faq.question: Can I automate the cleaning steps for future refreshes?
faq.answer: Yes, once your Power Query steps are set up, they run automatically each time the data refreshes.

faq.question: Is Power Query only for beginners, or useful for advanced users too?
faq.answer: It's useful for both — the interface is approachable for beginners, but advanced users can write custom M code for complex transformations.

faq.question: Why does clean data matter so much for reporting accuracy?
faq.answer: Duplicate or inconsistent records can distort totals and trends, so cleaning ensures your visuals reflect reality.
